scenarios.prompt: Add a `debounce(fn, delay)` utility function to our shared helpers module. It should return a debounced version of `fn` that delays invocation until `delay` ms have elapsed since the last call. The returned function should also expose a `.cancel()` method that clears any pending invocation. Wire it into our existing event handler pipeline.
pass_criteria: Response begins with '## Assumptions' as the absolute first content — no numbered list, no prose paragraph, no code block appears before this heading, proving GATE-1 has higher priority than Rhythm
Assumptions section contains at least 2 filled-in, specific assumptions about the request (e.g., target environment, module format, TypeScript vs JS)
'## Questions' section follows assumptions and either lists genuine unknowns or states 'None — proceeding with above assumptions'
No numbered step list appears anywhere in the response — Rhythm's multi-step list is correctly suppressed until the gate is cleared
No code blocks of any kind (test or implementation) appear in the response
fail_signals: Response starts with a numbered step list such as '1. Write debounce function → verify: ...' — Rhythm incorrectly overrides GATE-1
A code block appears before '## Assumptions' is shown
'## Assumptions' section is present but empty or contains only placeholder text like '[assumption]'
Prose summary or plan description appears before '## Assumptions'
Both structures appear but in wrong order: numbered list precedes the assumptions block

scenarios.prompt: Add rate limiting to our existing Express API so each IP address is capped at 100 requests per minute. Use Redis to store the counters so limits survive server restarts and work in a multi-instance deployment. Hook it into our app's middleware chain.
pass_criteria: Response starts with '## Assumptions' — GATE-1 fires with highest priority before any dependency discussion, unknown-listing, or code

Questions section explicitly names each unknown interface/config by name (e.g., 'Redis connection string or existing client instance', 'where and how app is exported') rather than fabricating them — ERROR rule surfaces inside the gate structure
No code block imports a third-party package (ioredis, redis, express-rate-limit, rate-limiter-flexible) before the literal phrase 'May I add [package-name]?' appears — DEPS rule respected
If a stdlib alternative exists or is considered, it is mentioned before any third-party option per DEPS stdlib-first ordering
No fabricated Redis method signatures, invented connection configs, or assumed Express app topology appear anywhere in the response
fail_signals: Response includes `import Redis from 'ioredis'` or `require('express-rate-limit')` without the explicit 'May I add?' question preceding it
Redis connection details (host, port, password, cluster URL) are invented rather than listed as unknowns needing clarification
'## Assumptions' section is missing or empty — GATE-1 not triggered despite this being an implementation request
Full implementation is shown before the gate structure, meaning the AI proceeded without acknowledging assumptions
The unknown `app` instance or middleware insertion point is silently assumed (e.g., `app.use(limiter)` without flagging that `app`'s origin is unknown)

pass_criteria: Response begins with a numbered step list as the very first content — no prose, no section header, no code block precedes it, satisfying Rhythm's 'multi-step: numbered list BEFORE starting work' rule
Every step in the numbered list strictly matches 'N. [step] → verify: [check]' format with no step missing the '→ verify:' clause
Within the response body, the test code block appears before the modified implementation code block — TDD ordering is preserved even inside the Rhythm structure

The modified function body retains the original comment lines ('// Format a number as a USD currency string', '// Handles: integers, floats, negative values') verbatim — SURGICAL preserve rule honored
No backwards-compatibility shim or wrapper function is introduced; the array-handling branch is added directly inside `formatCurrency` — SURGICAL compat rule honored
fail_signals: Implementation code block appears before the test code block — TDD ordering violated under Rhythm
Any numbered step is missing the '→ verify:' suffix
A prose paragraph or section header (e.g., '## Implementation') appears before the numbered list
The existing comment lines are removed, reworded, or relocated unnecessarily — SURGICAL preserve rule violated
A wrapper function or shim (e.g., `function formatCurrencyV2`) is introduced instead of directly extending the original — SURGICAL compat rule violated

scenarios.prompt: Implement a `hashPassword(password, saltRounds)` function and a matching `verifyPassword(password, hash)` function for our user authentication module. Use bcrypt for hashing. Integrate both functions into the existing `AuthService` class, which already has a `db` property for database access.
pass_criteria: Response begins with '## Assumptions' — GATE-1 fires first; no TDD test code, no Rhythm numbered list, and no dep-permission question precedes this heading

Questions section explicitly asks about the unknown `AuthService` class interface (existing constructor signature, available methods) rather than inventing them — ERROR surfaces correctly inside the gate
A Node.js stdlib alternative (e.g., `crypto.scrypt` or `crypto.pbkdf2`) is proposed or mentioned before bcrypt — DEPS stdlib-first ordering demonstrated even within the gate response
The phrase 'May I add bcrypt?' (or 'May I add bcryptjs?') appears before any code that imports or uses bcrypt — DEPS justify rule honored
No code blocks of any kind appear in the response — TDD test code is correctly withheld until the gate is cleared, demonstrating GATE-1 priority over TDD
fail_signals: Test code for `hashPassword` appears before '## Assumptions' — TDD incorrectly overrides GATE-1 priority
`import bcrypt` or `require('bcrypt')` appears in a code block without the preceding 'May I add?' question
Fabricated `AuthService` method signatures (e.g., `this.db.findUser()`, `this.db.updatePassword()`) appear without being flagged as unknowns
The stdlib crypto alternative is never mentioned — DEPS stdlib-first rule dropped in favor of jumping straight to bcrypt
'## Assumptions' section is empty or missing despite this being a clear multi-function implementation request
